text: ANGISTIO, an islet in the gulf of Achens, E from Port Piada on the main, in N lat. 37° 41'. It is about 7 m. in circuit. It is the ancient Pytenesus.
ANGITOLA, a river of Naples, in Calabria Ultra, descending from the Apennines into the gulf of Santa Eufemia, to the NE of Pizzo.
ANGLADE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Saint-Ciers-la-Lande. Pop. 1,279.
ANGLARDS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Salers. Pop. 2,400.
ANGLARS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Rignac. Pop. 1,350.
ANGLE, a parish of S Wales, in Pembrokeshire, on the E side of Milford haven. Pop. 388.
ANGLEFORT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Seyssel. Pop. 1,107.

ANGLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, cant. of Segonzac. Pop. 194.—Also a cant., com., and town in the dep. of Tarn. Pop. of cant., comprising 3 com., in 1831, 3,844. The town is 15 m. SE of Castres. Pop. 2,795.—Also a com. in Vendée, cant. of Les Moutiers. Pop. 1,060.—Also a com. in Vienne, cant. of Saint-Savin. Pop. 1,682.

ANGLESEY, or ANGLESEA, an island and county Mayenne, and Sarthe; it is also the seat of an assize- of North Wales, lying in the Irish sea, and separated from Caernarvonshire by a narrow strait called Menai, running nearly NE and SW, and from a half. to three-quarters of a mile broad. In general form it is triangular; but the shores are irregular, being indented with numerous small bays and creeks. In length it extends about 20 m. from NW to SE; its breadth is about 17 m.; its superficial area is estimated at 271 sq. m., or 173,440 acres. This county was originally divided into three cantrefs; but since the junction with England, the division into hundreds has been adopted. These are six in number: Llyfon, Malltraeth, Menai, Talybolion, Twercelyn, and Tyndaethwy; and are subdivided into 73 parishes. In ecclesiastical matters it is in the dio. of Bangor. The pop., in 1700, was estimated at 22,800; in 1801 it was returned at The increase on the pop. of this co. between 1801 and 1811 appears to have been 10 per cent., while the average increase for Wales within that decennial period was 13 per cent. Between 1811 and 1821, it was 21 per cent., the average for Wales being 17 per cent.; between 1821 and 1831, it had fallen to 7 per cent., the average for Wales being 12 per cent.; and between 1831 and 1841 it was 5.3 per cent., while the average for Wales was 13. The number of inhabited houses in 1831 was 9,688; in 1841, 11,487. The amount of poor rates levied in 1844 was £21,476, being at the rate of 8s. 5d. per head on the pop. in 1841; the average rate for Wales being 8s. 1d.; and for England and Wales 8s. 7d. The towns are Beaumaris, a sea-port and borough, with the market-towns of Llangefni, Newborough, Llanerchymedd, Holyhead, and Amlwch. There are several ports, as Beaumaris, Amlwch, Cremlyn, Dulas, Red Wharf, Malltraeth, and Holyhead. A number of small streams descend from the higher grounds, the most important of which are the Cefni, Alau, Fraw, and Dulas.—The climate of A. is mild; but the vicinity of the sea renders the district liable to fogs in autumn, when intermittent fevers are not uncommon. The general aspect of the country is uninteresting, the scarcity of fuel having caused much of the wood in the interior to disappear: the only exception is the shore of the Menai strait, which is picturesque and well-wooded. In an agricultural point of view, the land is in general good and fertile. The products are corn and black cattle; of the former—chiefly barley and oats—large quantities are sent to Liverpool. A. has long been celebrated for its black cattle, which are much prized in the English markets. It has also a considerable trade in butter, cheese, hides, tallow, wax, and honey; but scarcely any manufactures. The most important productions of the island, next to the agricultural, are the mineral. The rich copper and lead veins in the Parys mountain have been a source of increasing wealth and prosperity to the island. The annual value of real property within this co. was returned in 1815 at £92,589. In 1842-3 it was returned at £165,323; of which £129,063 was on lands; £15,231 on houses; £15,114 on tithes; and £5,833 on mines. The great line of communication between London and Dublin passes through this county. ANG The ancient name of Anglesey was Mon, signifying 'remote,' from which the Romans gave it the name of Mona. Its present name [Angle's-ey] was given by the Saxons, and signifies 'the Englishman's island.' It is generally supposed to have been the chief seat of the Druids. In the year 61, the island was invaded by the Romans under Suctonius Paulinus, who cut down the groves, and suppressed the order of Druids; whose complete extirpation was not effected, however, till some years after by Julius Agricola. From that period Anglesey remained under the dominion of the Romans, till they withdrew from Britain. A sovereignty was established here in 450 by Caswallon Llawhir, a British prince. After the subjugation of the island by Edward I., he built a castle at Beaumaris to intimidate the inhabitants. A. was afterwards incorporated with England; and in the reign of Henry was constituted a county, sending two representatives to parliament. Since
ANGLET, a commune and town of France, in the Basses-Pyrenées, cant. of Bayonne. Pop. 2,588.
ANGLEUR, a commune of Belgium, 3 m. S of Liege. Pop. 893. There are iron-works here.

ANGLEZARKE, a hilly district and township of Lancashire, bordering on Rivington-pike [alt. 1,545] ft.], in the parish of Bolton-le-Moors. Area 2,570 acres. Pop. 164. Houses 28. Carbonate of barytes was at one time quarried in this township.
ANGLURE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Marne, arrond. of Epernay. Pop. of cant., comprising 21 com., 7,821 in 1831; of com. and town, which are 25 m. SSW of Epernay, 722.
ANGMERING, a parish in Sussex, 3 m. ENE of Little Hampton. Area 3,640 acres. Pop. 1,002.
ANGO, or Angosero, a lake of Russia, in the gov. of Archangel, in N lat. 65° 48', E long. 33° 2'.
ANGOI, a district of Lower Guinea, between Loango and the Zaire river.
ANGOISSE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Lanouaille, 22 m. SSE of Nontron. Pop. 1,308.

ANGO'LA, a country of Lower Guinea, on the W coast of Africa, formerly called Abunda, or Ambonda, afterwards Dongo, and now Angola, a name given it by the Portuguese, or, according to Douville, by a Congo chief who, having conquered this territory, bestowed upon it his own name. Douville adds that it is still called by the neighbouring tribes Ririmi Abunda. In mercantile language, the whole coast between Cape Lopez Gonsalvo, S lat. 0° 44', and San Phelipe de Benguela, S lat. 12° 14', including Loango, Congo, Angola Proper, and nearly the half of Benguela, is denominated the Angola Coast; while the whole of the interior is called by the natives Congo. The kingdom of Angola Proper, between 8° 20' and 9° 15' S lat., and 14° and 19° E long., is bounded on the N by the Danda river which separates it from Congo; on the E by Malemba; on the S by the Coanza which separates it from Benguela; and on the W by the Atlantic. It is about 350 m. in length from E to W, and 50 or 60 m. in breadth, and probably has an area of 18,000 or 19,000 sq. m. It is divided into four provinces, viz., Quitama, Sumbi, Dembi, and Ovando. It is a mountainous country, with few plains, except towards the shore. M. Douville says he visited an active volcanic mountain called Zambi to the W of the Coanza. The same traveller says he visited a great lake called Cooffooa—i. e. 'Dead sea'—whence the river Cuzuila issues, and running W joins the Coango after a course of 150 m. The climate is unhealthy. The heat upon the coast is excessive; upon the mountains in the interior it is more moderate.— The principal rivers mentioned by the Portuguesefrom whom we have learned nearly all that we know concerning this country-are the Coanza and the Danda. The Coanza is supposed to originate, like the Zaire, in a great lake on the confines of the Cassanges territory. It is a rapid stream, having a course of 200 leagues, navigable by small vessels for about 45 leagues. Its current at its mouth is so violent that it makes the sea appear muddy to the distance of two or three leagues. Of the Danda, little is known. It falls into the sea 70 or 80 m. to the N of the Coanza. The other rivers are the Bengo, the Caibo, the Nica, and the Catacombola.—A. is rich in the varied productions of the three kingdoms of nature. Extensive fields of the finest cotton are skirted by noble forests of excellent timber; groves of acacia are intermingled with gigantic euphorbia; and large plantations of aloe stand side by side with splendid cacti. The indigo plant everywhere grows spontaneously; and coffee, said to be of a finer quality than that of Brazil, also grows wild. The environs of Loanda appear to be the least fruitful part of the country. Dr. Tams was informed that cloves, cinnamon, and nutmegs grow in abundance on Prince's island; and on some parts of the coast. Manioc, rice, and maize are generally cultivated; wheat and rye are said to be indigenous in the forests. Ebony, mahogany, packwood, various kinds of dye-woods, and the finest timber, are abundant. The fruit-trees are dates, palms, bananas, ananas, and oranges. Lions, tigers, leopards, hyænas, wolves, monkeys, and the civet-cat are amongst the wild animals; the zebra, elephant, rhinoceros, and hippopotamus are likewise found in this country; and its rivers swarm with crocodiles. Iron, copper, sulphur, rock-salt, asphalte, and gypsum occur. Pigafetta was told by Lopez that there was "infinite store of silver" in the hills of Cabambe about 110 m. inland, and more of other kinds of metal "than in any other country of the world whatsoever;" but none of the precious metals are known to be wrought in A. at the present day. The exports consist of ivory, gums, drugs, and wax.—The inhabitants of Angola are represented as having been formerly very numerous; but of their numbers we have no certain information. In some Portuguese works the pop. of the gov. of A. is stated at 400,000, of whom 12,000 are whites; while that of the whole territory of A. is stated at 2,000,000. They speak a dialect of the Congoese. Douville has given a comparative vocabulary of the two in his 3d. vol., which is probably as little worthy of confidence as the rest of his information. Loanda or St. Paul-de-Loanda, on the coast, in S lat. 8° 46', E long. 13° 9', the cap. of all the Portuguese settlements in Southern Africa, was founded in 1578; since which time it has been the residence of the Portuguese governor, who took the title of governor of Angola, instead of that of Congo. Its principal commerce is with Brazil and Lisbon.—The island of Loanda, separated from the continent by a narrow strait, is about 7 leagues in length, and one quarter league in breadth. The citizens of St. Paul-de-Loanda have their country-houses and gardens upon it, the air being reckoned salubrious. All this part of the coast of South Africa is exceedingly capable of yielding abundance of luxuriant productions; but it is divided into numerous petty states and sovereignties, the chiefs of which live in constant collision with each other. Pigafetta, writing towards the end of the 16th cent., says: "The king of A. was in times past but a governor and deputy under the king of Congo; although since that time he has be- ANG come a good Christian, and hath made himself a free and an absolute prince." Since the restriction of the traffic in slaves, rapine and cruelty have reigned with uncontrolled sway over the whole of this fine district; and since 1815, it is calculated that upwards of 100,000 of the natives have been annually transported as slaves. Abstracted from the unsocializing and debasing influences of the slave-traffic, the inhabitants of this part of South Africa are addicted to habits of idleness and polygamy. See article Congo. Authorities.] Pigafetta's account of Congo, contains a chapter on Angola, which the author says he drew up from the mouth of one Lopez a Portuguese. A translation of it, with a map of Congo by Abraham Hartwell, is given in the 2d volume of the Harleian collection. At a later period Fathers Angelo and Carli gave to the world an account of Congo as visited by them in 1666-7. They appear to have been much occupied with the spiritual objects of their mission. Father Merolla's voyage to this country in 1682 added little more to the geographical information collected by his predecessors. The narratives of these fathers are given both in Churchill's and in Pinkerton's collection. The adventures of Audrew Battel, who was kept in captivity in Angola by the Portuguese nearly eighteen years, supplies a good deal of matter. His narrative is contained in Purchas's collection, and is reprinted in Pinkerton's 16th vol. Later information respecting this region may be gathered from Degrandpré's Voyage a la Cote occidentale d'Afrique, fait dans les années 1786 et 1787; -Bowditch's Account of the Discoveries of the Portuguese; —Owen's Narrative; —Captain Tuckey's Expedition to the Zaire; -and Tams' Visit to the Portuguese Possessions in SW Africa, translated by Lloyd. Lond., 1845, 2 vols., 8vo.—A book of more pretension than all these is the Voyage au Congo et dans l'interieur de l'Afrique equinoxiale, fuit dans les années 1828-30. Par J. B. Douville. Paris. 3 vols. 8vo., 1832.

ANGOLA'LLA, a town of Abyssinia, in N lat. 9° 361 [Beke], to the W of the Chakka mountains; at an alt. of 8,400 ft. above sea-level. [Ibid.] It is a day's journey to the SW of Ankobar; and the king of Shoa resides here during several months of the year. [Krapf.] In journeying from Ankobar to A., Dr. Johnston describes the road as descending for the first half-hour to the banks of the river Airahra flowing into the Hawash; immediately after crossing which he commenced "a most villanous ascent,"—a series in fact of zigzag parallels up the face of the perpendicular sides of the Chakka range. On gaining the summit, an extensive table-land spread before him, on which he found "a Scotch climate and Scotch vegetation,"—a bleak moor-like treeless scene with numerous fields of wheat and barley, through which the road lay in broad shallow water-worn channels. After a ride of 7 hours, the straggling but extensive town of A. burst upon him, as he rounded the low shoulder of a ridge which had been in sight for nearly an hour. Three extensive but low hills of nearly equal height, and covered with houses, enclose a triangular space which forms the centre of the town. The palace stands on a ridge of red ferruginous basalt which has protruded through the general surface rock of grey columnar porphyry.

ANGORA, or Enguri, also called Ammu'riya', a sanjak of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Anadolia, between the Aladag mountains on the N; the Kizil- Irmak river on the E; and the Sakaria on the S and W. It is about 100 m. in length, and 60 m. in breadth; and contains the kadiliks of Angora, Yurukan, Ayash, Baji, Chibukabad, Chukurjuk, Murtezabad, Yerkoi, Shorba, Yabanabad, Haimaneh, and Korel. It is a very mountainous district, but presents extensive tracts of pasture-land. Kinneir says he could perceive neither villages nor cultivation when, from the top of a hill, the city of Angora, distant about 12 m. ENE, suddenly broke upon the view. The dogs, hares, lynxes, cats, and goats of this country are all remarkable for the length and silk-like fineness of their fur. Both Pococke and Kinneir were informed that the Angora goat is only to be found within the bounds of Wullikhan on the W, and Halys on the E; and that if carried beyond these limits, the hair immediately loses its fineness. Each goat produces on an average from 200 to 300 drachms annually. These animals are mostly white; but some are of an ash colour, and a few are black. The hair or wool grows in long curled ringlets. [Pococke.] ANGORA, ENGUR, or ENGURI, the ancient Ancyra of Galatia, the capital of the above sanjak, is situated in N lat. 39° 50', E long. 32° 45½' [Rennell], 212 m. ESE of Constantinople, and 12 hours from Kalahjik. It is pleasantly situated "on one of two steep rocky hills which rise up in the middle of a plain, enclosed on the N and E by mountain-ranges, and through which a small head-stream of the Sangarius flows to the W." The castle—which is like a small town in point of extent-occupies a strong position on the summit of the southern rock, which is perpendicular on three sides, but slopes towards the S. It is commanded, however, by an adjacent mountain, and has been suffered to fall into a state of dilapidation; and the city walls, built or repaired by Bajazet, are in the same mouldering condition. Tournefort, who visited A. in 1701, describes it as "one of the finest cities of Anatolia." The inscriptions at that time extant within the city were very numerous, and he copied several. Browne, who saw it in 1797, says-"It is one of the neatest cities I ever visited." The houses are principally of wood and brick, two stories high, with projecting verandahs and pent roofs; and the streets are wide, and even paved with blocks of granite. The town contains several fine mosques, and seven churches. The number of inhabitants has been variously estimated by different travellers at from 20,000 to 100,000; but probably does not exceed 20,000, of which number about one-third are Armenians of the Catholic persuasion. [Kinneir.] Browne praises the polished manners of the inhabitants. The trade of A.—which has greatly declined in activity of recent years—is almost entirely in the hands of the Armenians and the Catholics, who constitute about one-third of the pop. They exchange for the cloth and colonial produce of Smyrna the fine coloured camlets manufactured of the tiftik or beautiful silk-like hair of the goat peculiar to the province. The weavers of these shal or shawls, and sof or camelots, are principally Armenians. An inferior kind are manufactured at Ayash, but the finest sorts only in A. The caravans are 20 days on the road from A. to Smyrna, and 10 days to Brussa. The environs of Angora have a soil of a fine red marl [Browne], and produce grain, opium, honey, and wax, and a good red wine; and are celebrated for their fruits, particularly a delicious species of pear, which is cultivated in the gardens on the NW of the city. This plain—the scene, most probably, of the great battle between the vast armies of Tamerlane and Bajazet, on Friday, the 28th of July, 1402— is watered by the Chibuksu, which proceeds from a lake on the NE of the town, and, after encircling the rock on which the castle is situated, and forming an artificial cascade over a bund or dyke, continues its course to the Sangarius.—Ancyra, the ancient name of this city, is supposed to have been derived from an anchor discovered in its foundation, and long kept as a sacred relic in the temple of Jupiter. Its early history is lost in obscurity. Pausanias ascribes its origin to Midas. It became the capital of the Tectosages, one of the tribes of the Galli or Galatea, who had established themselves in the surrounding country. It afterwards assumed the name of Sebaste, in honour of Augustus, who raised it to the rank of metropolis of the province, and adorned it with many stately edifices. The gratitude of the inhabitants for the favours of this emperor, induced them to pay him divine honours, under the representation of their favourite god Lunus, and to erect a splendid marble temple to his memory. [Mem. de l'Acad. des Inscript., Tome xxviii.] When Galatia was divided Dec. 17 1819. ANG into two provinces by Theodosius, A. contained the capital of one of the sections. In 625 it was finally lost to the Roman empire during the invasion of the princes of the house of Seljuck. In 1102 it was taken by the Count of Thoulouse, but was recovered by Sultan Amurath in 1359. In 1402, when Tamerlane vanquished Bajazet, A. opened its gates to the conqueror; and in 1405 it was constrained by Mahomet I. to relinquish its ancient name for the Turkish appellation by which it is now distinguished. The history of A. explains the existence of the numerous relics of Roman sculpture which it exhibits; its gates. walls, and all its later buildings, are constructed of fragments of its ancient edifices, many of them models of antique art. The form of the ancient city appears to have been elliptical. Within the walls are the ruins of a magnificent white marble temple (supposed to be that dedicated to Augustus) with a vestibule, richly inscribed with the principal actions of that emperor's life; and at a little distance is a mosque with some beautiful columns. A. has been characterised as one of the most pantheistic of cities, from the facility with which it has given admission to every species of religious worship, from Druidism to the purest Christianity. It is supposed to have been one of the scenes of the ministry of St. Paul. It is now the seat of an Armenian bishop.—Tournefort's Voyage du Levant.—Pococke's Travels.—Browne's Travels in 1792- 8.—Kinneir's Journey in 1813-14.—Hamilton's Asia Minor.—Geog. Journal, vol. vii.
ANGORI, a town of Anatolia, on the E coast of the sea of Marmora, 38 m. SW of Nicomedia.
ANGORNU, a large and populous town of Bornu, a few miles W of Lake Tchad, in N lat. 12° 37'. Pop. said to be upwards of 30,000.

ANGOSTA, or ANGOZHA, a district and town of Portuguese Africa, on the Mozambique coast. It is said to be a highly fertile district; and is watered by a river of the same name which might be made navigable for large vessels. The shores are fringed with low coral islands, two of which, Barrow and Caldeira, are sometimes known as the Angora islands.
ANGOSTO (PUERTO), a harbour in the strait of Magalhaen, 9 m. from Point Ildefonso.
ANGOSTURA, a town of Paraguay, on the Paraguay river, 20 m. S of Assumption.—Also a town of New Grenada, 180 m. N of Santa Fe de Bogota, on the r. bank of the Magdalena.

ANGOSTU'RA (SANTO TOME' DE), a town and cant. of Venezuela, in the prov. of Nueva Guayana, 700 m. E of Santa Fe de Bogota, on the Orinoco, and 370 m. from its mouth, in N lat. 8° 8', W long. 63° 55'. Pop. about 4,000; in 1807 it was estimated at 8,500. The houses are in general well-built, and the streets are regular. The usual breadth of the river opposite the town is 3,130 ft.; but in floods it lays a large part of the town under water. Its elevation above sea-level is only 191 ft. It is finely situated for trade; and, but for the distracted state of the country, would prove a thriving place, and command the whole inland trade to a great distance westwards, and also the trade with the West Indies, especially Trinidad, from which it is 15 days by sailing vessels; but this will probably be reduced to from 60 to 70 hours by the steamers of the Orinoco steam-packet company. Its exports chiefly consist of cocoa, indigo, cotton, sugar, hides, and jerked meat.—The second congress of Venezuela, commonly called the congress of Angostura, was held in this city, Feb. 15, 1819; in the 9th year of the independence of Venezuela. This congress had the honour of proposing and effecting the union of New Grenada and Venezuela into one government, under the name of Colombia, by the fundamental law of the republic, dated

ANGOTE, a province of Abyssinia, to the E of Amhara. This country has fallen into the hands of the Gallas; and Angote has not been visited by Europeans since 1520, when Alvarez went through it with the Portuguese embassy.

ANGOULE'ME, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of La Charente. —The arrond. has an area of 197,912 hectares; with a pop. in 1836 of 130,456; and in 1846 of 136,653. It comprises the 8 cant. of Angoulème, Saint-Amant-de-Boixe, Blanzac, Hiersac, Montbron, La Rochefoucauld, Rouillac, and La Valette.—The cant., comprising 22 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 37,252. The town of Angoule'me, the cap. of the dep. of La Charente, is situated at the confluence of the Touvre with the Charente, on a rocky eminence which rises to the height of 221 ft. above the Charente, 287 m. SSW of Paris, in N lat. 45° 39', E long. 0° 10'. The acclivities of the city are ascended by several flights of stairs; and the terrace which occupies the place of the ancient ramparts commands one of the most extensive and beautiful prospects on which the eye can rest. The town is of an irregular oval form; and is divided into two distinct parts, the old and the new town. The former is gloomy and uninteresting, with narrow, winding, ill-built streets. The Quartier Neuf descends in a southerly direction from the castle, and is rapidly increasing in extent. The principal public buildings and establishments are the cathedral, considered one of the best specimens of the Roman architecture of the 12th cent.; the church of St. Andrew; the college; the castle, of which the most ancient portion is the great round tower built towards the middle of the 12th cent.; the chapel of St. Gelais; the house of the prefecture; and the justiciary court. It possesses a public library of 15,000 vols. In the suburb of St. Cybard is a fine bridge over the Charente. Situated on the Charente, its port renders A. the general entrepot of the surrounding country. Its commerce consists chiefly in grain, wine, brandy, essences, spices, hemp, lint, truffes, saffron, soap, cork, iron, copper, &c. It has some manufactories of serge and coarse cotton fabrics; and its paper-mills in the environs are celebrated. It also has spinning mills, sugar refineries, tanneries, tile-kilns, and distilleries of brandy. About 1 m. from the town are extensive powder-mills. It has two markets every week; a monthly fair which is held in the Place du Champ de Mars; and three royal fairs every year, which last 8 days. A. is the seat of a bishopric, comprehending the dep. of the Charente, of a court of assize of first appeal, and a chamber of commerce. The pop. in 1789 was 12,350; in 1846, 18,482.—The origin of A. is lost in antiquity. It was the capital of the Agésinates, the ancient inhabitants of Angoumois; and the Iculisma of the Romans. From the domination of the Romans it passed to that of the Visigoths, who retained it till 507, when it was taken possession of by Clovis, after the battle of Vouillé. Destroyed by the Normans in the 9th, and rebuilt in the following cent., it long remained under the oppression of feudal tyranny. After having been reunited to the Crown, it was ceded to the English after the battle of Poictiers, but was subsequently rendered by Charles V. an appanage of the royal family of France. In 1515 A. and its territory were erected into a duchy. It afterwards passed to the house of Guise; and in 1710 was reunited to the Crown by the death of the Duke of Angoulême.—A. has the honour of being the birth-place, in 1492, of Margaret of Valois, queen of Navarre; of the poet Mellin de Saint-Gelais in 1491; of J. L. Guet de Balzac, a restorer of the French language, who died in ANG died in 1775; and of the engineer, Rene de Montalembert, who died in 1800.
ANGOUMER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariège, cant. of Castillon. Pop. 869.

ANGOUMOIS, an ancient division of France, comprising Angoumois Proper, L'Aunis, and La Saintonge. Of its area of 1,177,594 hectares, 525,798 are now included in the dep. of La Charente; 578,818 in La Charente Inferieure; 42,728 in Deux- Sevres; and 30,250 in Dordogne. Angoumois Proper has an area of 486,572 hectares, and comprises the towns of Angouleme, Cognac, Jarnac, La Rochefoucauld, and Chabanais. The prov. of A. formerly bore the title of a county; and after passing through different hands, by the peace of Bretigny in 1360, was ceded to England. The inhabitants, however, soon rebelled against their new masters, and put themselves under the protection and dominion of Charles V. of France. A. subsequently became an appanage of the younger line of Valais. Francis I. before his accession to the throne, bore the title of Count of Angouleme. In 1515, he erected it into a duchy in favour of his mother; after whose death it reverted to the Crown. Henry II., in 1552, gave it to his daughter Diana, who died without issue. Louis mortgaged it to Charles of Valais, a natural son of Charles IX.; after whose death, in 1563, it came a second time to the Crown; but it was held for a considerable period by the Dukes of Joyeuse, till 1775, when the title of Duke of Angouleme was conferred on the eldest son of the Comte d'Artois.

ANGRA, a port on the S coast of Terceira, one of the Azores, of which it is also the cap., in N lat. 38° 38', W long. 27° 15'. The situation of this town is very fine. Its streets are regular, and its houses wellbuilt. Its central situation and harbour have occasioned it to be selected as the seat of the local government, and it may be considered as the cap. of the entire group. The English, French, and Dutch maintain consuls here for the protection of their commerce. Its chief commerce is in fruit and grain. The pop. amounts to about 8,000. The bay and town are protected by strong fortifications. The bay is open to all winds from SSW to E.—Also a village of Lower Guinea, on a river of the same name flowing into a bay formed by Cape St. John and Cape Esteiras.

ANGRA DOS REIS, a town of Brazil, prov. of Rio Janeiro, on a bay of the same name, SW of Rio de Janeiro. It contains about 250 houses arranged in a crescent form upon a plain bordered by mountains. Its pop. is about 2,000. It has a good harbour

ANGRA PEQUENA, or SANTA CRUZ, a bay on the W coast of Africa, 17 leagues S of Spencer's bay, in S lat. 26° 37', E long. 15° 20'. The surrounding country is a monotonous expanse of sandy desert without a shrub; but at about 50 m. to the E it becomes fertile and inhabited. The missionary station of Bethany lies four or five days' journey—between 60 and 70 m.—to the N of this coast in S lat. 25° 45'. The bay is well-sheltered from NW winds, and abounds in fish, especially mullet. Inside Penguin island there is good shelter.
ANGRAB, or Bassalam, a river of Abyssinia, which, rising near Tcherkin, in Samen, and, being joined in its progress northward by the Andeval, falls into the Tacazze, on the border of Sennaar.
ANGRANO, a village of Lombardy, prov. of Vicenza, 1 m. NW of Bassano. Pop. 2,800.
ANGROGNA, a district of Piedmont, in a valley surrounded by vast mountains, on a river to which it imparts its name. This was the last and most secure retreat of the persecuted Waldenses.

ANGUE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Cande. Pop. 1,205. ANGUIJAN, ANATAJAN, or ANATACAN, one of the Ladrone, or Marian islands, in the North Pacific, in N lat. 14° 30', E long. 146° 0'. It is about 10 m. in circumference, mountainous, and uninhabited.

ANGUILLA, or SNAKE ISLAND, a low, flat, irregular shaped island, the northernmost of the Caribbee or Leeward islands, in N lat. 18° 8', W long. 63° 12'. It is separated from St. Martin's by a channel of which the least width is 4 m. and depth from 13 to 20 fath. It is about 20 m. in length, and 6 m. in breadth. Its 1654; of Vivien de Châteaubrun, the dramatist, who | northern extremity rises to nearly 40 ft.; but the surface falls from this ridge towards the S. The productions are sugar, cotton, maize, salt, yams, and cattle; but seven-tenths of the surface are still uncultivated. The climate is salubrious. Pop. in 1825, 3,080, of whom 2,388 were Negroes. The governor of the Leeward islands reported, in 1846, that the native inhabitants of A. are a superior race, and particularly correct in their deportment; that crime is almost unknown amongst them. It is a dependency of St. Kitt's, and sends one representative to the assembly of that island. At its eastern end is an islet called Anguillita.—Also one of the Bahama islands, in the Bahama old channel, at the eastern extremity of the Anguilla bank, in N lat. 23° 26'.

ANGUILLARA, a town in the States-of-the- Church, on Lake Bracciano, where the Arrone issues from it, 12 m. NW of Rome. - Also a town of Lombardy, on a lake of the same name, near the r. bank of the Adige, 6 m. NNE of Rovigo. Pop. 2,000.
ANGUILLE, a bay and cape on the W coast of Newfoundland, in the gulf of St. Lawrence, 10 leagues NNW from Cape Race; in N lat. 47° 54'.
ANGUS. See FORFAR.
ANGVIG, a village of Norway, prov. of Drontheim, on the S shore of the estuary which communicates with the North sea at Christiansund.

ANHALT, a territory in the north of Germany, composed of two principal and six smaller and noncontiguous portions; containing a total superficial area of about 965 sq. m. It is enclosed on all sides, except on the NW-on which it adjoins the ducal state of Brunswick—by the Prussian province of Saxony; and, except in the direction of the Hartz mountains, presents a nearly level surface, watered by the Elbe, which intersects it from E to W, and by its tributaries the Mulde and the Saale, and several minor streams. It is considered one of the richest districts in Germany: producing corn, flax, fruit, hemp, tobacco, timber, cattle, iron, coal, copper, and silver. It is divided into the three duchies of Anhalt-Dessau, Anhalt-Bernburg, and Anhalt-Cothen; and contains 27 towns, and 341 villages and hamlets, with a pop. of 152,000, of whom 130,000 are Protestants. Each duke—who is a prince of the house of A.—exercises sovereign authority in his own dominions; but primogeniture entitles to precedence in succession, and in the direction of affairs common to the duchies. They are jointly responsible for the public debts; and vote collectively with Oldenburg and Schwartzburg in the ordinary diets of the Germanic confederation, but possess distinct votes in its plenary assemblies. The duchies provide a federal contingent of 1,266 men. Their united revenues amount to £150,000.—The territory of A. is supposed to derive its name from he ancestral seat of its ancient principality, the stronghold of Anhalt on the Hartz, said to have been built by Count Esico, to whom the ducal house traces its origin, and who, in the 11th cent., possessed the castle of Ballenstein and the surrounding country. The territory acquired by his descendants received a great augmentation from the marriage of Otto with the heiress of Duke Magnus of Saxony. His son, Albert the Bear, possessed Lusatia, Brandenburg, and a part of Thuringia, all of which he divided amongst his seven sons, one of whom, Bernhard, was the ancestor of the present dukes of Anhalt. In 1252 the principality was divided into three parts: Aschersleben, Bernburg, and Zerbst. After the death of Joachim Ernest, a new partition of the territory was made by his sons, in 1606, into four principalities, distinguished by the names of their capitals, viz. A.-Dessau, A.- Bernburg, A.-Cethen, and A.-Zerbst. The latter ceased to exist in 1793; and with the exception of the seigniory of Jever-which was inherited by Catherine IL—was divided in equal portions amongst the other ANH principalities. Early in the present century these principalities were erected into duchies.—J. C. Beckmum, Historie des Fürstenthums Anhalts. Zerbst 1710. 7 vols. fol.

ANHALT-DESSAU, the most extensive and important of the three duchies, consists of four detached portions, containing a superficial area of 15.32 German, or 344 English sq. m. The largest connected tract, that of Dessau, stretches on the l. bank of the Elbe, and on both sides of the Mulde. Besides these rivers, this duchy possesses the Nuthe, Fuhne, Tauber, Wipper, Bode and Ziethe, or Zittau; and several lakes, the most considerable of which are the Pötnitz, near the confluence of the Mulde and Elbe, Lake Shönitz, and the picturesque Lake Worlitz. The other parts of the duchy are Zerbst, Sondersleben, and Gross-Asleben. Its administrative division is into five amts or bailiwicks: viz., Dessau, Oranienbaum, Qualendorf, Gröbzig, Sondersleben, Gross-Asleben, and Zerbst. The country is a perfect plain, generally well-wooded, but presenting very different degrees of fertility. It abounds in excellent pasture-land. The district on the l. of the Elbe is the richest, and is as finely cultivated as a garden; on the other side, the soil is meagre and unproductive. Some tracts, covered with sandy heaths, occur in Dessau Proper. The climate is mild and salubrious. Besides the productions common to Germany, this duchy affords an excellent species of apple, and considerable quantities of madder. Its principal exports are grain, porcelain, and salt. The most important industrial occupations of its inhabitants are agriculture and the rearing of sheep and cattle. Great care is here bestowed upon the improvement of the breeds of cattle; pigs are numerous, but horses are comparatively scarce. The only manufactories exist at Dessau and Zerbst, and consist of woollens, paper, leather, tobacco, salt, oil, and porcelain. The revenue is said to amount to 710,000 florins; Balbi states it to have been about £76,000, and the debt £85,208 in 1826. The contingent to the army of the confederacy is 529 men. The pop. in 1843 was 62,690, of whom 1,600 were Jews, and 1,100 Roman Catholics. The religion of the great majority is Lutheran Protestantism. The duchy is well-supplied with very efficient educational establishments.—It is intersected by the railway from Leipsic to Magdeburg. Its cap., Dessau, is a handsome town, with 12,000 inhabitants, situated on the Mulde. The towns next in importance are Zerbst, formerly a ducal residence, now containing a pop. of 9,000; and Vorlitz, with a pop. of 1,900, in which there is a fine castle with magnificent gardens belonging to its ducal sovereign.—Many of the princes of the line of Anhalt-Dessau distinguished themselves in the service of the electors of Brandenburg, then kings of Prussia. In 1807 the title of Duke was assumed by the prince regnant on his entering into the confederation of the Rhine. In 1815 he furnished nearly 1,200 men to the Germanic confederacy. The prince of this little district is almost an unlimited sovereign. The landstadt is only summoned at his pleasure. The duchy of Anhalt-Bernburg is divided by the Prussian territories into several detached portions, situated on the l. bank of the Elbe, and between the Hartz mountains and the Saale. It contains a total area of 14.19 German, or 319 English sq. m.; and is divided into the Hartz territory or Upper duchy; and the territories of Bernburg, Mühlingen on the Saale, and Koswig on the Elbe, which compose the Lower duchy. Its administrative division is into nine amtsbezirks or bailiwicks, of which Ballenstedt, Hoym, Gernrode, Hartzgerode, and Guntersberge are in the Upper duchy; and Bernburg, Plotzkau, Muhlingen, and Koswig, are in the Lower. Some of the bailiwicks are also subdivided into seigniories. The upper principality is mountainous and woody, is intersected with some fertile vallies, and extremely picturesque. The lower district, which stretches on the l. bank of Saale, and on the r. of the Elbe, and is crossed by the minor streams of the Fuhne, the Wipper, and the Bode, with its tributary the Selke, presents a level surface with a fertile and richly cultivated soil. This duchy derives its principal wealth from its agricultural productions, which consist of every variety of grain and green crops, flax, hemp, and tobacco. Fruits are reared in abundance, and there is an increasing quantity of wine produced. The mountains afford silver, copper, lead, iron, sulphur, alum, gypsum, marble, and freestone. The duchy possesses few manufactures besides those for home-consumption, except iron-smelting works. Its principal exports are iron and iron-ware, timber, wool, corn, and butter. Cattle, sheep, and pigs are reared in great abundance; but the horses are chiefly of foreign produce.—Game and fish are plentiful; of the latter the salmon of the Saale are particularly celebrated. The temperature of the upper district is considerably colder than that of the lower. The revenue amounts to about £49,000, and the debt to £85,000. The pop. of the duchy in 1843 was 48,844, of whom 400 were Jews. Its federal contingent is 370 men; but not more than 120 are in active service. Its educational establishments are well-regulated and supported; and they and the clergy, who are principally Calvinist, are under the direction of the consistory of Bernburg. - Bernburg contains 7 towns, and 54 villages. Its cap., Bernburg, in the lower district, is situated on the Saale, and contains a pop. of 6,000. In the upper principality are the ducal residence of Ballenstedt, with 4,000 inhabitants; Gernrode, Hoym, and Hartzgerode, celebrated for their metallic works and mineral waters. In the neighbourhood of Wilhelmhof, a farm belonging to the duke, are the ruins of the ancient castle of Anhalt, built by Count Esico in 906. In 1806 the title of duke was conferred by Francis II. upon the reigning prince. The duchy of Anhalt-Cethen, which comprises a superficial area of about 300 sq. m., consists of four discontiguous portions, of which two, Cethen and Roslau, situated on the r. side of the Elbe, are, with the exception of a few sand-hills, flat and unfertile; the other two, Warmsdorf, on the Wipper, and Dornburg, are fertile. The administrative division is into six amts, viz., Coethen, Reinsdorf, Nienburg, Wülfen, Warmsdorf, and Roslau. Besides the Elbe, this duchy is watered by the Saale, Bode, Wipper, and Mulde. The principal occupations of the inhabitants are, like those of the other duchies, agriculture, and the rearing of flocks and herds. The climate is temperate. There are quarries of lime, gypsum, freestone, and magnesia. The revenue is about £35,000; and the debt about £125,000. The pop. amounts to 42,106. The contingent is 355 men. Of the 47 parishes into which Coethen is divided, 28 belong to the Reformed, and 19 to the Lutheran churches. There are I Roman Catholic church, 2 synagogues, 2 general schools and 1 normal; and several libraries and other scientific establishments. The seigniory of Pless, in Upper Silesia, containing a pop. of 43,000 and producing an annual revenue of 49,000 florins, is still in the possession of the duke of Coethen, who also possesses an inalienable property in the Russian gov. of Taurida, amounting to 225 sq. m. - The cap., Coethen, situated on the Zittau, possesses 6,000 inhabitants.
ANHANDAHY, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, rising in the Serra Amambubu, and flowing into the Parana in a course from NW to SE. ANI
ANHAUSEN, a town of Rhenish Prussia, in the gov. of Coblentz, circle of Neuwied. Pop. 323.
ANHAY, a town of China, in the prov. of Fokien, in N lat. 24° 58'.
ANHEL (PONTE DE), a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Entre Douro e Minho, on the Veiva, 4 m. N of Barecellos.

ANHOLT, or ANHOLDT, an island of Denmark, in the Kattegat, in N lat. 56° 38', E long. 11° 35', between Jutland and Helmstadt in Sweden. It is flat, sandy, and very low; and has an area of about 1,000 acres, and a pop. of about 150 souls, who are chiefly collected in the v. of Anholtbye, and supported by fishing. It was taken by the British in 1809, and the Danes were repulsed in an attempt to retake it in 1811. A dangerous reef called the Knoben reef, extends 5½ m. E ½ S from it.—Also a small town on the Ouden-Yssel in Westphalia, 12 m. W of Borken. Pop. in 1837, 1,876. There is a castle here belonging to the prince of Salm-Salm. ANI, Anni, or Anisi, the ancient Abnicum. A town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Kars, near the r. bank of the Arpachai, about 8 m. to the S of its confluence with the Kars, and 30 m. to the E of the town of Kars. In the 11th cent. it was the cap. of Armenia; but is now only remarkable for the magnificence of its ruins. In 993 it became the residence of the patriarchs of Armenia. In 1045 it fell into the hands of the Greeks; and about 20 years later it was taken by Alp-Arslan. It afterwards passed successively under the dominion of the princes of Georgia, Persia, Armenia, and of the Moughols. In 1319 it was entirely destroyed by an earthquake. Colonel Monteith, who visited the ruins of A. in 1831, found the walls still perfect, except where breaches had been made by the Tartars. Of the churches, six were still perfect—Geog. Journ.
ANIAN. See Behring's Strait.

ANIANE, or St. Benoit, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Herault and arrond. of Montpellier.—The cant. comprises 7 com. and a pop. in 1831 of 5,709.—The town lies at the foot of a mountain-ridge, near the l. bank of the Herault, 16 m. WNW of Montpellier. It contains the ruins of a monastery founded by St. Benoit in the reign of Charlemagne, and about 400 houses. Its principal manufactures are crystallised tartar, verdigris, black soap, cotton, yarn, and leather. Pop. of com. and town 2,480.

ANIBA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Guayana, which rises in the Aruaquis territory, runs in a SE direction, and, after receiving the waters of Lake Saraca, forms, at its confluence with an offset of the Maranon, in S lat. 2° 50', the island on which the town of St. Yves is situated.

ANICHE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Nord, and arrond. of Douay, 8 m. E by S of Douay. Pop. 1,926. Its principal manufactures are glass, sugar from beet-root, and iron. The environs abound with coal and iron.
ANICUL, a town of Hindostan, near the SW frontiers of Mysore, about 18 m. S of Bangalore.

ANICUNS, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, which rises in the Sierra Escalvada, and, after its confluence with the united streams of the Paranaibo and Gurumbo, tracks the confines of the prov. and joins the Rio Grande in S lat. 18° 50'.

ANIEH, or Hanifah, a town of Arabia, in the Nedjed, 340 m. SW of Lachsa, on the great caravanroad from that town to Mecca. It is famous as being the birth-place of Abd-ul-Waheb, the prophet of the Wahabees. Its environs produce excellent grapes, dates, and peaches.
ANIERES. See ASNIERES.

ANILLE, a river of France, in the dep. of Sarthe. which takes its rise in a small lake on the southern confines of the forest of Vibiage, runs in a southerly direction, passes St. Calais, and, after a course of about 14 m., unites with the Braye above Bessé.
ANILORE, or AMILORE, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, which rises in the mountains of Cacoa, and, running to the N, falls into the Madeira, in about 5° 5' N lat., and 60° 40' W long.

ANIMALLY, or ANIMALAYA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Coimbatur, on the l. bank of the Alima, 18 m. S of Coimbatur; in N lat. 10° 35′, E long. 76° 59′ 30". It consisted in 1800 of about 400 houses; and from its situation between the southern extremity of the Eastern Ghauts and the northern end of the ridge which runs southerly to Cape Comorin, it was the common thoroughfare between the Carnatic and Malabar districts. The surrounding forests abound with valuable timber. Elephants are numerous in the environs; whence the name of the town, Ani Malaya, i. e. 'Elephant hill.' The adjacent territory is subdivided into twelve petty states.
ANIMANCY, or ANIMCI, a town of Marocco, near the base of the Jebel-Telge in the Atlas range, 30 m. E of Marocco.

ANIMAS (RIO DE LAS), or JAQUIN, a river of Mexico, in the prov. of New Mexico, which is supposed to have its source in the mountains of Anahuac, and which runs to the SSW, and unites with the Nabajoa about 70 m. above the junction of the Rio Colorado.
ANINEE, a river of Guinea, which rises in the territory of Akim, and enters the Bosempra in that of Assim, after a course from E to W of 48 m.
ANINSK, a village in Russia, in the gov. of Perm, consisting of about 200 houses. In the environs is a copper-mine, which employs 300 workmen, and produces annually 250 tons of metal.
ANIO. See AGNO.
ANIOIA, or Anoja, a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra, 11 m. SE of Nicotera.
ANISA, or ANIZEH, a town of Arabia, in the district of Kasym, 270 m. ENE of Medina.
ANISI. See Ani.
ANISY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Creully. Pop. 500. In the environs is a remarkable intermittent stream.

ANISY-LE-CHATEAU, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Aisne, arrond. of Laon.—The cant., comprising 22 com., had a pop. of 10,791 in 1831.—The com. is 8 m. W by S of Laon, on the Lette river. Pop. 1,000. ANIUJ, (GREATER and LESSER), two rivers of Siberia, tributaries of the Kolyma. The Greater A. appears to rise near the head-streams of the Anadyr, but flows in an opposite direction, and enters the Kolyma by three mouths opposite to Nishni Kolymsk. The Lesser A. flows into the Greater A. on the r. bank. It is wider and more rapid, but not so deep as the Greater A. Both streams flow through districts covered the greater part of the year with snow, but here and there presenting patches of birches, poplars, willows, and aspens. The Jukahiri inhabit the banks of these rivers, and gain a precarious subsistence from fishing and hunting the reindeer.

ANIVA (BAY OF), or ANIWA, or TAMBAOUORA, a great indentation at the southern extremity of the island of Sachalien, in N lat. 46° 41', E long. 142° 32', extending from the cape of the same name on the E, to Cape Crillon on the W. The shores are steep, and are skirted by a range of precipitous, snow-capped hills, which on the E open towards the sea into woody vallies.
ANJA, a lake of Chinese Tartary, in Little Bukharia, 70 m. S of Khoten. It is said to be 60 m. in circuit; but little is known of it. ANN

ANJAR, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Aleppo, between Aleppo and Skanderun.-Also a district of Hindostan, prov. of Cutch, near the gulf of that name, 20 m. SE of Booj. Its capital, of the same name, is in N lat. 23° 3', E long. 70° 11'. Pop. 10,000. It was taken by the British in 1816. In 1819 it suffered severely from an earthquake.
ANJEDIVA, or ANGEDIVA, an island in the Indian ocean, in N lat. 14° 44', E long. 74° 15'. It is about 1 m. in length, and is only 2 m. distant from the main.

ANJENGA, or Anjengo, a town of Hindostan, prov. of Travancore, on the coast, 8 m. S of Coulan; in N lat. 8° 30', E long. 76° 53'. It has some trade in pepper and calicoes. The SW monsoon sets regularly in here on the 12th of April.
ANJIER, a large village on the N coast of Java, 78 m. W of Batavia.

ANJOU, an ancient province of France; bounded on the N by Maine; on the W by Brittany; on the S by Poitou; and on the E by the Touraine. Its superficial extent was about 893,838 hectares. Its cap. was Angers. It now forms the dep. of Maine-et- Loire, and part of the dep. of the Sarte, the Mayenne, and Indre-et- Loire. Forty-nine rivers, of which the Loire, Vienne, Fouey, Mayenne, Sarte, and Le Loir, are navigable, water this variegated tract. It was divided into Upper and Lower Anjou. The province of Anjou was retaken in 1203 by Philip Augustus of France, from the English who had held possession of it since the beginning of the preceding century. St. Louis bestowed it, in 1246, on his brother Charles, the head of the first line of Anjou; but it fell again to the Crown in 1328. John I. raised it to the rank of a ducal peerage, and gave it to his son Louis I., the head of the second Sicilian house of Anjou; but it reverted once more to the Crown in 1480. Different princes of the blood subsequently bore the title of Anjou, till Louis XV. conferred it, along with the province, on his grandson, Louis Stanislaus, Count of Provence, afterwards Louis XVIII.
ANJOUAN. See ANZUAN.
ANKAPILLY, a town of Hindostan, in the Northern Circars, on the shores of the bay of Bengal, in N lat. 17° 41', E long. 83° 11'.—There is a small town of the same name 20 m. W of Rajamundry.
ANKAWA, a village of Asiatic Turkey, in the gov. of Bagdad, in a fine country watered by tributaries of the Zab.
ANKER, a river rising near Hinckley, in Leicestershire, and joining the Tame at Tamworth.

ANKLAM, or ANCLAM, the chief town in the circle of the same name, in Pomerania; 2 m. W of the Frische-haff; 20 m. SE of Griefswald. Both the inland and the maritime commerce of this town are considerable. It was ceded to Prussia, by Sweden, in 1720. It suffered severely during the Seven years' war, and had its works demolished in 1762. Pop. in 1837, 7,434.

ANKO'BER, the capital of the prov. of Efat, in Abyssinia; in N lat. 9° 34′ 33″, E long. 39° 33′; 174 hours or 372 m. travelling distance from Tajurrah; 300 m. SE of Gondar. It is surrounded by a high mountainous country, in which it is situated at an elevation of 8,200 ft. above sea-level, on two wooded hills, the northern one of which is strongly palisaded, and forms exclusively the residence of the king. A. is now the residence of the prince of Shoa, who has rendered himself entirely independent.
ANKOKO, a town of China, in the prov. of Quantung, at the S extremity of the isle of Hainan, in N lat. 18° 4′, E long. 109° 11′.
ANLIER, a town of Luxemburg, 11 m. NW of Arlon, on the high-road to Neufchateau.
ANLOY, a village of Luxemburg, 12 m. NW of Neufchateau, on a branch of the Lesse.
ANM, a town of Sweden, in the prov. of Calmar, on a river of the same name.
ANMER, a parish of Norfolk, 6 m. N by E of Castle-Rising. Area 1,420 acres. Pop. 175.
ANN (CAPE), a headland forming the northern side of the bay of Massachusetts, opposite to Cape * Cod. Two lighthouses on an island off this cape are in N lat, 42° 40', W long. 70° 38'.

ANN (FORT), a township of Washington co. New York, U. S., 68 m. N by E of Albany. Pop. 3,559. The village of Fort A. is on the Champlain canal, which has here its summit-level. Fort A., which gives its name to the township, at the head of the batteaux navigation on Wood creek, was celebrated in the French and the Revolutionary war.

ANN (Sr.), a lake in Upper Canada, to the N of Lake Superior; communicating through Albany river with James's bay. Its NE extremity is in N lat. 50°, W long. 88°.—Also a river of Lower Canada, which joins the St. Lawrence about 50 m. above Quebec, by a channel 1,200 ft. broad, but, on account of shallows and falls, it is navigable only with great danger .- Also a small river falling into the St. Lawrence, opposite to the island of Orleans.—Also a town of New Brunswick, on the St. John's river, 80 m. above the town of St. John.—Also a parish in the isle of Man. Pop. 769.

ANN (CAPE ST.), a headland on the coast of Western Africa, S of Cape Blanco, in N lat. 20° 35′, W long. 16° 30'.—Also a cape, forming the NW point of Sherbro' Island, in N lat. 7° 34', W long. 12° 57'.
ANNA. See ANA.
ANNA, or CURRENT ISLE, a small islet in the Pacific ocean, 90 leagues SE of the Pelew islands, in N lat. 4° 40′, E long. 131° 50′.
ANNA (FORT), a fortress on the island of Sumatra, at the mouth of the Si-Luggan, opposite to Mocomoco, in S lat. 2° 45'.

ANNABONA, an island off the coast of Western Africa, in the Atlantic, nearly 50 leagues SW by S of the island of St. Thomas; in S lat. 1° 25', E long. 5° 42'. It is of a conical form, and about 30 m. in circumf. It produces wood, water, cattle, sugar, cotton, oranges, limes, rice, Indian corn, and yams. The pop. is said to amount to nearly 2,000, most of whom are Negroes.
ANNA DE CHAVES, a town on the island of St. Thomas, in N lat. 0° 25′ 30″, E long. 6° 46′, on a bay of the same name.
ANNA DE SAPOCAHY (SANTA), a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas Geraes, 43 leagues SW of San Joaö del Rey.
ANNA DOS ALEGRES (SANTA), a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas Geraes, near the confluence of the Catinga and Paracatu rivers; 58 leagues NW of Villa do Principe.

ANNA (Santa), a lagune in Guatemala, prov. of Tabasco, near the gulf of Mexico, with which it communicates by the creek of Santa Anna.—Also an island in the straits of Magalhaen, on the coast of Patagonia.—Also an island in the Atlantic ocean, on the coast of Brazil, in the bay of San Joze, in S lat. 2° 19', W long. 43° 4'.—Also a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, on the Degebe, 18 m. SSE of Evora.—Also a small town of Old California, on the shore of the gulf of California, between Capes Arenas and St. Lucas.—Also a town of Peru, in the prov. of Cuzco, on the Yacuma river.—Also a village of Peru, in the prov. of Cuzco, on the Quillabamba.— Also a town of Bolivia, on the Paragau, in S lat. 16° 40'.—Also a lofty mountain on the peninsula of Paraguana, on the coast of Colombia.—Also a lagune on the coast of the gulf of Mexico, 31 m. S from the mouth of the Tupilco river.-Also a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Porto-Seguro, running into the Rio di San Matheus.—Also a town in the prov. of Matto Grosso; 150 m. NNW of Villa Maria.—Also a small port of European Turkey, on the island of Amorgo, in the Greek Archipelago; 30 m. of Naxia.-Also a town of Hungary, 8 m. WNW of Siri. Tobacco is ANN lage of Greece, in the prov. of Salonica, on the coast, at the base of Monte Santo, in N lat. 40° 7'. ANNA (ISLE OF SANTA), OF BANNANAL ISLE, a considerable tract of country in Brazil, insulated by one of the branches of the Araguaya which separates from the main stream in about S lat. 12° 30', and rejoins it in S lat. 9° 30'.
ANN-ABBOTS, a parish in the co. of Southa pton, 2 m. SW of Andover. Area 3,100 acres. Pop. 619.
ANNABELOE, an island off the W coast of Norway, in N lat. 58° 12', E long. 6° 38'.

ANNABERG, a town of Saxony, in the circle of the Erzgebirg, 25 m. SW from Freyberg. Alt. above sea-level 2,800 ft. It had a pop. in 1697 of 5,000; in 1834, of 6,697, most of whom are employed in working lace, or in the mining of silver, tin, cobalt, and marble in the environs.—Also a town in the duchy of Saxony, which belongs to Prussia, on an island formed by a branch of the Black Elster, 18 m. from Wittemberg. Pop. 1,700.—Also a town of Austria, in the circle of Wienerwald, 30 m. SE of St. Poelten. Pop. 700. Cinnabar is mined here.
ANNABUOY, a river of Ireland, in co. Cork, flowing eastward in a course of 15 m., into the head of Crosshaven estuary in Cork harbour.
ANNACLOY, a river of Ireland, in co. Ulster, flowing into Lough Strangford after a SE course of about 20 m.
ANNADUFF, a parish in co. Leitrim. Area 10,970 acres. Pop. 6,162.
ANNAGELIFFE, a parish in co. Cavan, 1 m. NE of Cavan. Area 8,260 acres. Pop. 3,808.

ANNAGH, an island on the W coast of Ireland, about 5 m. in circumference, between the isle of Achil and the mainland of co. Mayo, in W long. 9° 39', N lat. 53° 58'.—Also a small island of Lough Conn, in co. Mayo, 8 m. from Killala.—Also the name of three parishes in different parts of Ireland; 1st, in co. Kerry. Area 13,735 acres. Pop. 3,659; 2d, in co. Cavan. Area 19,145 acres. Pop. 13,071. It is ecclesiastically divided into A. East and A. West; 3d, in co. Mayo. Area 20,315 acres. Pop. 7,904.
ANNAGHDOWN, a parish in co. Galway, 7 m. N of Galway. Area 23,730 acres. Pop. 7,108.
ANNAGH UAN, or Annachnan, a small island of Ireland, on the SW coast of Galway, 22 m. W from Galway; in N lat. 53° 18', W long. 9° 38'. It is about 3 m. in circuit.
ANNALEE, a river of Ireland, flowing into the Erne, 2½ m. below Butler's Bridge, after a course of 28 m. chiefly W.
ANNALONG, a fishing-village in co. Down, 5 m. NE by N of Kilkeel.

ANNAM, or ANAM, an Asiatic empire, comprising a great portion of the peninsula between Hindostan and China, or the regions generally known as Cochin- China and Tonquin, the eastern and southern part of Cambodia, and several islands in the Chinese sea, along which it extends upwards of 1,200 m. It lies between 8° 40' and 23° 20' N lat., and 105° and 109° E long.; having the Chinese provinces of Quangtong, Quangsi, and Yunnan on the N; the Chinese sea on the E and S; the gulf of Siam on the SW: and Siam and Laos on the W. It is nearly 1,000 m. in length from N to S; and its breadth varies from 400 to 50 m. Crawford estimates its area at 98,000 sq. m.; Balbi, at 120,000 sq. m. The pop. has been variously estimated at from 5,000,000 to 10,000,000. Bissachere's estimate of 23,000,000 is certainly an enormous exaggeration. The country is naturally divided into two long stripes of territory by a chain of mountains running in a direction nearly parallel to the coast, from the frontiers of China to the mouth of the Maykiang or cultivated here in considerable quantities.—Also a vil- river of Cambodia in N lat. 9° 35'. The Tonquin and Cochin-Chinese territories lie to the E of this range, and Cambodia to the W. These countries will be found described in our pages under their respective names. A brief historical notice of the empire of Annam as now existing will form the remainder of the present article. Tonquin anciently formed part of the Chinese empire; but on the Mogul invasion of China, in the 13th cent., the Chinese governors of the south set up the standard of independence; and in this manner several distinct kingdoms were created, the sovereigns of which, however, continued to acknowledge a nominal vassalage to the throne of China. Cochin- China was separated from Tonquin about 600 years ago, since which period it has received a variety of names from different geographers. It is said to have been indebted for its population to an unsuccessful rebellion of a Tonquinese prince against his sovereign. Totally routed, and pursued by the victorious troops of the king of Tonquin, he made his escape with his adherents into Cochin-China, then inhabited by the Loys and Kemoys, an ignorant and timid people, who fled with precipitation to the mountains of Tsiampa on the approach of the intruders, and left the Tonquinese fugitives in quiet possession of their country. The fertility of the soil, and the profusion of animals, fowls, and fish, with which the woods, marshes, rivers, these means, in a short time they had spread themselves over all the northern section of the country; nor was it many years ere they had penetrated southwards as far as the borders of Cambodia, where they built the city of Saigon, and subsequently that of Donnai, about 30 m. to the northward of Saigon. Thus, tants was greatly facilitated by the nature of the country, which being low, covered with impenetrable forests or with thick underwood or jungle, and intersected by innumerable rivers and creeks, afforded them many advantages in the desultory modes of warfare in use among barbarous nations; nor were the Cambodians finally subdued by the hostile arms of the Annamese until the reign of the father of the present sovereign, who rendered Cambodia an integral part of his empire. In the year 1774, the misrule of the reigning dynasty in Cochin-China brought on a formidable insurrection, in which they were expelled from Quinnong, the capital, by three brothers, peasants by birth and robbers by profession, who divided the country amongst them, and defeated a Chinese army which had been sent to the assistance of the monarch. When the revolt took place, the reigning prince, Gia- Loung, with the queen and his family, by the assistance of a French missionary named Pignon de Behaim, titular bishop of Adran, escaped into a forest where they lay concealed for some time. After various unsuccessful attempts to regain his throne, he was compelled to fly to Siam. The bishop in the meantime proceeded to France with the eldest son of the dethroned prince, to endeavour to procure assistance from the court of Versailles, but this object was frustrated by the breaking out of the Revolution. He returned, however, to Cochin-China in 1790, bringing with him fourteen or fifteen European adventurers, by whose assistance the king formed a fleet, disciplined an army, and at length managed not only to expel the successors of the original usurpers from his her- I. editary possessions, but to add to them the richer and more populous country of Tonquin. With the same assistance he began many improvements seldom attempted by Asiatic governments. He established a manufactory of saltpetre, opened roads of communication, and encouraged cultivation. He distributed his land-forces into regular regiments, and established military schools in which officers were instructed in the doctrines of projectiles and gunnery by European masters; while Adran translated into the Chinese language a system of military tactics, for the use of his army. In the course of two years, he had a fleet consisting of 300 large gun-boats, or row-gallies, 5 luggers, and a frigate on the model of an European vessel. He also undertook to reform the system of jurisprudence, and sent missions into the mountainous districts of the west which he wished to bring into a state of civilization. He openly declared his great veneration for the Christian religion; but adhered to the ancient religion of his own country. In 1800, the missionary, Adran, died, and was interred with all the pomp and ceremonies prescribed by the Cochin- Chinese religion. In that year, King Gia-Loung's military forces were as follows:- ARMY. Men. 24 squadrons of buffalo troops, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . manner, Infantry armed with matchlocks, trained in the ancient manner of the country, 42,000 Guards regularly trained in European tactics, . 12,000 Land forces, 113,000 MARINE. Artificers in the naval arsenal, Arthrees in the naval arsenal,
Sailors, registered and born, on the ships in the Total, In 1809, taking advantage of the dissensions which prevailed in Cambodia, he, partly by force of arms, and partly by intrigue, acquired the most valuable part of that country, and thus established at once the most extensive and best-organised power which had ever subsisted in this part of the East. The genius which created it, however, was, as we have seen, substantially French. The political system of the Annamese government—like that of all the countries of India beyond the Ganges—is one of extreme caution and aversion to any intimate connexion with neighbouring or foreign powers. The pretensions of China to the kingdom of Tonquin, formerly tributary to that empire, are incessantly to be guarded against; but while the Annamese sovereign supports his present military force, and perseveres in his improved system of government, he has little to fear from any of his immediate neighbours. In all the more recent wars between the Cochin-Chinese monarchs and the Chinese, the latter have been uniformly discomfited by the superior valour and discipline of the troops opposed to them. At the same time, the king is still a nominal vassal of the Chinese emperor; and every new prince receives investiture by a deputation from the court of Pekin. Gia-Loung died in 1819; his son, the present sovereign, has reduced his standing-army to 40,000 men; and concentrated his strength upon the fortifications of the city of Hué, which are reported to mount 1,200 guns, the smallest of which are 18 and the largest 68 pounders. The foreign relations of Annam are with China, Siam, Macao, and the British possessions within the straits of Malacca; but the inland trade with China is probably more considerable than that by sea. From China, Annam receives manufactured silks, English broad cloths, and Bengal opium, with the copper, spelter, and lead of Yunnan; its returns are cotton, areca-nuts, varnish, dye-stuffs, and a variety of other native products. Two attempts have been made by the East India company to open an intercourse with Cochin-China, -one in 1778, by Mr. Hastings, and one in 1804, by an envoy from Canton; but both proved unsuccessful. The last found the sovereign completely surrounded by Frenchmen; and, as every proposition he had to offer, and every explanation regarding his mission, were necessarily made through the French missionaries, the result—as might have been anticipated—was complete failure in the object of his mission. Louis XVIII., in 1817, sent an embassy to this country claiming somewhat unreasonably the fulfilment of the treaty of 1787, no part of which had been fulfilled on the part of the French themselves. The proposal gave umbrage to the monarch, who would not even enter upon a discussion of the subject; and his successor is said to have shown so little encouragement towards the French adventurers that they have finally quitted the kingdom. Of late years the chief part of the trade of the Cochin-Chinese is said to have been with the Portuguese at Macao; but the coast is seldom visited by any foreign vessels except a few Chinese junks, and leave to trade can be obtained only at a high rate; and when that leave has been obtained, high duties are levied on the goods exposed, and presents exacted by those in power. In April 1847, a French fleet silenced the forts and destroyed five corvettes of the Cochin-Chinese in Turon bay, with the view of avenging the ill-treatment experienced by the French missionaries. Soon after this, an English mission undertaken by Sir John Davis, from Hong-Kong, was received with kindness but great caution. Our recent settlements on the NW coast of Borneo and on Labuan will doubtless operate to place our intercourse with the Annam territories on a much better footing than has hitherto been found practicable. The French have been long desirous of making a settlement, for the purpose of trade, on the island of Callao, not far from Turon bay. A settlement at that place would be of the utmost importance to Great Britain. Cochin-China is admirably situated for carrying on an extensive commerce with China, Japan, Cambodia, Siam, the Malay coast, the Philippine islands, Borneo, the Moluccas, &c.; and the first politic and prudent nation which fairly gets a footing here, may change the whole course of trade to the East, and lay the foundation of a commercial empire rivalling that which Great Britain at present holds in India. Turon is, of all others, the most advantageous position for an establishment, either in a commercial or in a political point of view. From this point, in case of war, the trade of every power, from the westward to the countries east of this, would be at the mercy of that establishment.

ANNAMABOE, one of the principal commercial places on the Gold coast of Africa; in N lat. 5° 10', W long. 1° 7′ 12″; 10 m. E from Cape Coast castle. Pop. from 4,000 to 5,000. The English fort here is the strongest on the coast. The soil along the seaboard is poor, but becomes fertile as it approaches the interior, and seems well-adapted for cotton and sugar. The highest land in the district is about 200 ft. above sea-level. The rains set in here in May and continue for four months. There are several large Fantee towns from 12 to 40 m. distant from A. which are well-adapted for trade, and in 1840 exported to the value of £60,000 in London and Bristol ANN ships. The imports average £50,000, and are sold in the town to Ashantee, Fantee, and other native traders. They consist of Manchester goods, silks, iron, copper, brass, lead, powder, guns, rum, tobacco, wines, beads, cowries, and cutlery. The exports are gold-dust, ivory, palm-oil, corn, pea-nuts, and Guineagrains.—Parl. Report.
ANNAMAPIA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Solimoens, flowing into the Maranon in S lat. 1° 30'.

ANNAMOOKA, or New Rotterdam, one of the Friendly islands, discovered by Tasman in 1643, and visited by Captain Cook in 1774 and 1777. It is in S lat. 20° 15′, W long. 174° 48′. It is about 12 m. in circuit, and is well-cultivated, in many places presenting plantations of yams and plantains enclosed with neat fences of reed. The bread-fruit and cocoa-nut tree are interspersed near the habitations of the natives; and the other parts of the island, especially towards the sea, are covered with trees and bushes. In the middle there is a lagune, 11 m. in breadth.

ANNAN, a royal burgh and parish in the shire of Dumfries, 79 m. south of Edinburgh, and 23 m. from Dumfries, by the Glasgow, Dumfries, and Carlisle railway, which has a station here, and joins the Caledonian line at Gretna. Area of parish 11,000 acres. Pop. 5,471. The town is situated on the Annan river, near the confluence of that stream with the Solway frith; and is well-built, containing several spacious streets, a town-house, and markets. Manufactures of cotton-ginghams are established here, and a productive salmon-fishery is carried on at the mouth of the river. Pop. of the town 4,409. A., in connection with Dumfries, Kirkcudbright, Lochmaben, and Sanquhar, sends one member to parliament.—The river Annan rises on the S side of Hartfell in Tweeddale, flows through Dumfries-shire, and falls into the Solway frith, after a course of 36 m. On the W it is joined by the Evan, the Ae, and the Kinnel; on the E by the Moffat, the Wamphray, the Drife, and the Milk. It furnishes trout, salmon, and other fish in great abundance; and is navigable for vessels of 250 tons, to within half-a-mile of A.
ANNANES, a group of small islands off the coast of Greece, 4 leagues SW of Milo, in N lat. 36° 38'.
ANNAPOL, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Volhynia, circle of Ostrog, 19 m. NE of Ostrog. Pop. 1,200.

ANNA POLIS, a town and port-of-entry in Anne eo., Maryland, U.S., on the SW bank of the Severn, 2 m. from its mouth; 28 m. SSE of Baltimore; in N lat. 39° 0′, W long. 76° 43′. Pop. in 1840, 2,792. It is the capital of the state; but the flourishing commerce of its near neighbour, Baltimore, tends to check its trade and prosperity. The university of Maryland has one of its branches here under a president and 4 professors.

ANNA'POLIS, a county of Nova Scotia, bordering on the bay of Fundy. Area 2,500 sq. m. It was first colonized by the French; but was taken by the British in 1614, and again in 1710. The settlement was then called Port Royal; but, on Acadia being ceded to the British in 1713, the province received the name of Nova Scotia, and the town that of Annapolis. This county contains the six townships of A., Granville, Wilmot, Clements, Digby, and Clare. Pop. in 1827, 14,661; in 1817, it was 9,721.—The cap. of the same name, sometimes called Annapolis

ROYAL, is on the E side of the bay of Fundy, 86 m. NW of Halifax, in N lat. 44° 40′, W long. 65° 37′. It has a magnificent harbour, 6 m. long, by 3 wide, with deep water; but the entrance is through a difficult strait, called the gut of Annapolis, with the inconveniences of rapid currents, high tides, and frequently dense fogs. The town stands on the S of the harbour, at the mouth of a river of the same name. It was ceded to the British in the reign of Queen Anne, from whom it derives its name. It was the seat of government until 1750, when the public offices were transferred to Halifax, the present capital.—The river A., rising 60 m. NW of Halifax, on the Aylesford plains, passes by the town of Annapolis, and, after a course of nearly 70 m., parallel to the bay of Fundy, falls into the harbour of A., having been previously joined by the Moose and Bear rivers. It is navigable 10 m. above its mouth for ships of any burden; and for vessels of 100 tons nearly 20 miles. The tide flows between 30 and 40 m. up this river; and, in the harbour of A., rises 30 feet. The banks on either side are composed of rich meadows, backed by high lands on the E and W.
ANNAPPES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Lannoy. Pop. 1,821.
ANNAPU, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, an affluent of the Tagypuru, one of the side-branches of the Amazon.

ANN-ARBOR, a town of Michigan, U. S., cap. of Washtenaw co. It occupies an elevated site on the r. or W bank of the Lower Huron, 36 m. W of Detroit, and is the seat of the university of Michigan. Pop. in 1838, 2,000.

ANNATOM, an island in the S Pacific, the most southerly of the group of the New Hebrides in S lat. 20° 15', and E long. 170° 12'. It is 36 m. in circumference; and is covered with mountains, some of considerable altitude. The productions are yams, bread-fruit, cocoa-nuts, and sandal-wood. The inhabitants are similar to those of the neighbouring island of Tanna. On the N side is a small port capable of containing 5 or 6 vessels; and 2 m. to leeward is another small harbour.
ANNAWUTTY, a village of Hindostan, on the NW confines of Mysore, 50 m. N of Bedenore.

ANNAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Lens. Pop. 1,108.—Also a rivulet of Guayana, flowing S into the Rupununi, and usually considered the boundary between the British and Portuguese possessions. The settlement of A. was ascertained by Schomburgk to be in N lat. 3° 56', W long. 58° 36' 15".
ANNAY-SUR-SERAIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Novers. Pop. 720.

ANNE (St.), a parish and the chief town of the island of Alderney, in the English channel, commonly called La Ville. Pop. 1,300, comprising nearly all the inhabitants of the island. To the E is a fort which defends the port; and to the Sa bay capable of affording harbourage to small vessels.

ANNE (SAINTE), a mountain of France, in the dep. of Orne, about 10 m. from Alençon, on which is a chapel famous for the miraculous cures which are alleged to be performed here on invalid devotees who annually collect within its precincts, to the number sometimes of 15,000.—Also a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, and cant. of Baccarat. Pop. 821. It is celebrated for its crystal-wares.—Also a hamlet of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, and com. of Labuissiere, on the Sambre. In the environs are extensive quarries of a fine species of marble, distinguished by the name of the village.

ANNE (SAINTE), a canton and town in the island of Guadaloupe, on the S coast of Grande Terre, about 12 m. ESE of Point à Louis.-Also a town of the Antilles, on the E side of the island of Marie Galante, about 6 m. NE of Grand Bourg. Pop. 6,727, of whom 5,866 are slaves.—Also a parish and town in the southern extremity of the island of Martinique. The district comprised by the former is intersected by hills cultivated to the summit. In dry seasons it is irrigated by means of tanks and cisterns. ANN Its sugar-houses produced a few years ago 31,900 quintals of raw sugar, and employed 2,241 Negroes. Pop. in 1841, 2,831, of whom 2,660 were slaves. The town, which is the cap. of the district, is situated on a small bay, on the W coast, about 3 m. to the N of the Point des Salines.

ANNE-ARUNDEL, a co. of Maryland, U. S., stretching along the W side of Chesapeake bay, and separated from the adjacent counties by the rivers Patapsco on the N, and Patuxent on the W. It presents an undulating surface, and is generally fertile. Its principal productions are wheat, oats, potatoes, swine, and cattle. It contains some iron-mines. The cap. is Annapolis. Pop. 29,532. ANNEBAUT, ANNEBAULT, or Appeville, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Eure and arrond. of Pont-Andemer, on the r. bank of the Rille, 5 m. ESE of Pont-Andemer, and 23 m. WSW of Rouen. Pop. 1,147.

ANNECY (LAKE), an inland sheet of water in Savoy, in the duchy of Genevois. It is about 10 m. in length from NNW to SSE; and varies in breadth from 1 to 21 m. It is elevated 210 ft. above the level of the lake of Geneva, and 1,460 ft. above that of the sea, its greatest depth is said to be 196 ft. It is surrounded on all sides, except the N, by steep calcareous mountains, of which the highest are on the E side, where the Tournetts rise from 5,560 to 5,637 ft. above the lake. A small river, called the Thion, forming its outlet, issues from its NE extremity; and after a short course unites with the Fier, which falls into the Rhone a little above Seissel. The only considerable stream which runs into the lake is La Rivière Morte, which rises beyond Faverge, and enters the lake at its S end, but its bed is nearly drywhence perhaps its name-except in the rainy season. The banks are richly adorned with large walnut and chestnut trees. Near the centre of the lake is the small island of Duing, connected by a causeway with the mainland, on which is a castle, surrounded with gardens and orchards of great luxuriance and beauty.
