At the southern extremity is a marshy plain, where the land is gaining on the lake, which once evidently filled up the whole of the valley. [Bakewell.] The lake contains few fish according to the report of some travellers; but abounds in them according to others. Among its peculiar species are the lotte, (Gadus lota,) first introduced in 1770, and the vaison.

ANNECY, a town of Italy, in the Sardinian government of Savoy, the cap. of the duchy of Genevois, and next in size and importance to Chamberry. It is situated at the NW extremity of the lake of the same name, 22 m. S of Geneva, and 94 m. NW of Turin; 1,456 ft. above sea-level. The surrounding scenery is rich and beautiful, and is enclosed on all sides by lofty mountain-ranges. The town, which is of considerable antiquity, contains some fine modern buildings, as well as numerous relics of its former importance. The houses are high, and as some of the streets are bordered with low heavy arcades, they appear extremely gloomy. Occupying a commanding position in the suburbs is a castle, now in ruins, which was the residence of the ancient counts of the Genevois; and in the neighbourhood is the castle of Menthon in which St. Bernard was born. A. possesses manufactures of cotton and silk fabrics, delft-ware, glass, hardware, vitriol, and straw-hats, besides flax, cotton, and silk spinningmills, and several calico-printing establishments. Its bleacheries, established in 1650, are still celebrated; and it has four annual fairs, held in May, July, October, and December. In the environs are some iron-mines, and a vein of coal is wrought at Entreserne. A. is the see of a bishop, a suffragan of the archbishop of Chamberry. It possesses a theological seminary, and is the seat of several public offices. This town in the 12th cent. was known as Anneciacum novum, and is supposed to be of Roman origin, and to have derived its name from Annicius, who was governor of the province. It was once ravaged by the Goths, and twice destroyed by fire, in 1412 and in 1559. Pop. 5,700.

ANNECY LE VIEUX, or IL VIEJO, a commune and village of Savoy, about 2 m. NNE and supposed to occupy the ancient site of the preceding town. Pop. 1,200. ANNELAND, ANNELARD, or ANNALAND, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Zieland, an island of Tholen, 7 m. NW of the town of that name, and 9 m. ESE of Zierikzée. Pop. 1,450.

ANNEMASSE, or ANEMAS, a commune and village in the Sardinian gov. of Savoy, 4 m. E of Geneva, on the road to Chamouni. It is the station of a frontier custom-house, and contains some Roman antiquities. Pop. 760. Alt. above sea-level 2,493 ft.
ANNEPONT, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of La Charente Inferieure, 8 m. SW of Saint-Jean-d'Angely. It has a considerable trade in gun-flints, which are made here. Pop. 423.
ANNESLEY, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 6 m. SSW of Mansfield. Area 3,360 acres. Pop. 274.
ANNESTOWN, a small port in co. Waterford, in the parish of Dunhill, 3 m. WSW of Tramore. There is a lead-mine in the neighbourhood.
ANNET, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Claye. Pop. 1,005.
ANNET, or Anney, an islet in the Scilly group, about 1 m. to the W of St. Agnes. It is now uninhabited, but it exhibits the ruins of houses considerably below sea-level.
ANNEVOYE, or Annevou, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, arrond. of Dinant, on the l. bank of the Meuse, 7 m. NNW of Dinant. Pop. 471. In the environs are iron-works.

ANNEYRON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of La Drome, and cant. of Saint Vallier, 25 m. N of Valence. Pop. 2,891. In the environs are the ruins of the castle of Mantailles, in which the council was held, in 879, which transferred to Boson the crown of Cisjurane Burgundy.
ANNEYULATE, a town of Abyssinia, in the territory of the Somauli Arabs, about 110 m. to the SW of Berbera; in N lat. 9° 44', E long. 44° 11'.
ANNI. See ANI.

ANNICO, or Anico, a town of Austrian Lombardy, prov. and 10 m. NW of the town of Cremona. ANNIVIERS (VAL D'), [GERM. Einfischthal,] a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, watered by the Navigenza, which takes its rise on the N side of the glacier of the Weisshorn, [14,812 ft.,] and falls into the Rhone opposite the town of Sider. This beautiful and romantic valley is about 20 m. in length. At its head it divides into the Val de Torrent and the Val de Zinal; and it expands at some points to a considerable breadth. The soil possesses considerable fertility. The inhabitants are a race distinct in origin, it is said, from their Swiss neighbours. Their houses and apartments are among the worst in the Alps; cleanliness is not amongst their virtues, nor industry it appears. Their principal occupations are the rearing of cattle, and the making of cheese. Copper, silver, and cobalt mines exist in the district; but they have been abandoned for want of the means of transport. Pop. 1,607. Perched on a rocky eminence, near the entrance of the valley, are the ruins of the strong castle of Perigord, the ancient residence of the lords of Raron. ANNŒULIN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Le Nord, and cant. of Seclin. Pop. 3,210. ANO
ANNOIRE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Le Jura, and cant. of Chemin. Pop. 946.

ANNONAY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, and arrond. of Tournon. The cant., which comprises 14 com., contained, in 1831, a pop. of 19,383. The town is situated at the confluence of the Cance and Déaume, 15 m. NW of Tournon. Its manufactures are numerous and important. The principal are those of glove-leather, the most extensive in France, producing annually to the value of 5,000,000 francs. There are also several paper-mills, silk and cotton spinning-mills, bleacheries, and wax and tallow candle works. A. is the entrepot of the manufactures of the surrounding districts, and possesses a chamber of commerce. It is celebrated as the birth-place of the brother-aeronauts Montgolfier, and of Boissy d'Anglas. Pop. 10,384.
ANNONE, a town of Italy, in the Sardinian prov. of Piedmont and district of Alessandria, on the l. bank of the Tanaro, 15 m. WSW of Alessandria, and 5 m. E of Asti.
